questions.question: Explain the two models of Inter-Process Communication with neat diagrams.
keyPoints: Define IPC and why cooperating processes need it
Shared Memory Model: processes share a common memory region; kernel sets up the region; processes read/write directly; faster but programmer must handle synchronization
Draw shared memory diagram showing two processes accessing a shared region
Message Passing Model: processes use send() and receive() operations; kernel manages message transfer; easier to program but slower per message
Draw message passing diagram showing processes communicating via kernel/mailbox
Comparison: speed, system calls, synchronization, suitability for distributed systems
Examples: shared memory for producer-consumer, message passing for client-server

questions.question: Differentiate between direct and indirect communication in message passing.
keyPoints: Direct: processes explicitly name each other -- send(P, msg) and receive(Q, msg)
Indirect: messages sent to/received from mailboxes -- send(A, msg) and receive(A, msg)
Direct has exactly one link per pair; indirect can have multiple links via different mailboxes
Direct has limited modularity (hard-coded names); indirect is more flexible
